title: SEGMENT 16 — Bioprinted cornea: a small organ with big stakes

lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — Bioprinted cornea: a small organ with big stakes
A related success story, on an even smaller scale, is the bioprinted cornea.
The cornea is the transparent front layer of the eye.
Damage to the cornea — from disease, injury, or chemical burn — is one of the leading causes of blindness worldwide.
Corneal transplantation works, and is one of the most successful organ transplant procedures, but donor corneas are in chronic short supply, particularly in developing countries.
A bioprinted cornea would solve this problem.
The cornea has a few features that make it especially well-suited to tissue engineering.
It is avascular — corneas have no blood vessels, which is part of why they're transparent — so the vascularization problem doesn't apply.
It is thin — about half a millimeter — well within the diffusion limit.
It has a relatively simple layered structure: an outer epithelium, a thick stroma made of regularly arranged collagen fibrils, and an inner endothelium.
Each layer has a distinct cell type with relatively well-understood biology.
In 2018, a team at the University of Newcastle led by Che Connon published a paper demonstrating the bioprinting of a corneal construct.
The bio-ink was a mixture of collagen and alginate, with embedded corneal stromal cells — keratocytes — derived from donor corneas.
The construct was printed using a low-cost extrusion bioprinter in under ten minutes.
The printed cornea had the proper curvature, the right thickness, and the right basic architecture.
The cells in the construct remained viable and started laying down corneal matrix proteins.
The work demonstrated that, in principle, a cornea-shaped tissue could be produced rapidly and reproducibly.

Several research groups, including teams in Glasgow, Boston, India, and China, have continued this work, with various improvements — better materials, better cell sources (including stem cell-derived corneal cells), better post-print maturation.
Clinical translation is in progress.
A bioprinted or partially bioprinted corneal product is likely to be one of the next tissue-engineered organs to reach approved clinical use, perhaps within the next several years.
The cornea story is a good lesson in scope.
Sometimes the right strategy for a young technology is not to attack the hardest problem but to attack a problem that's well-matched to your current capabilities.
The cornea is small, thin, avascular, and architecturally simple.
It is exactly the kind of tissue that bioprinting can reasonably tackle today.
The fact that one in fifty global blindness cases could be helped by a cheap, on-demand printed cornea is not a small thing.
It is, quietly, one of the field's likely first big public health wins.
